items.rationale: Sees fast, general SNARK proving as foundational crypto infrastructure; ships it as a public good and treats working code as the proof of conviction.

citations.quote: This is why we're developing ⚡️ Jolt, our open source proof of concept reference implementation free forever public good love letter to crypto ZK VM.
We've developed a new lookup argument called Lasso 🪢 that is already 10x faster than halo2, with a clear path to 40x.

items.rationale: Believes the internet is collapsing into mostly-generated content, and that the only serious solution to proving origin/history requires cryptography and blockchains.

citations.quote: Every robust method to prove the origin or history of content involves cryptography and blockchains.
Crypto skeptics should explain how they think we're going to solve this problem. I don't think it can be done seriously without crypto.

items.rationale: Core thesis: permissionless, censorship-resistant verifiable computation is what crypto is for, and centralization is the real limiter on innovation.

citations.quote: Once this is clear, they appreciate crypto-the-computer (vs. crypto-the-casino).
Remarkable how powerful it is to (1) give every user a cryptographic key that is (2) linked to a secure verifiable computer (blockchain).

items.rationale: Compute and data costs are the binding constraint on good crypto ideas; light clients and data-availability sampling reduce trust and cost.

citations.quote: we unpack danksharding and data availability sampling, which would radically decrease the cost of storing data on Ethereum
The same is true for crypto. 90% of the unfeasible but good ideas I hear are primarily limited by compute.

items.rationale: Repeatedly champions app/account-layer privacy and compliance-compatible privacy as a prerequisite for mainstream adoption.

citations.quote: And privacy is coming to crypto.
Cicada is the cleanest app layer private voting I've seen

items.rationale: Sees private decentralized proof-of-personhood as necessary once AI breaks CAPTCHA and floods networks with fake identities; complements trust/attestation graphs.

citations.quote: How bad does this need to get before all networks need to integrate a private proof of personhood system like @worldnetwork?
Trust graph is much more robust coupled with a sybil resistance mechanism, which is what Worldcoin provides.

items.rationale: Wants networks that resist centralization by design, where names, value and data live in a permissionless unified computer rather than corporate silos.

citations.quote: We need networks where key components (names, value, data avail., etc) live in a permissionless, unified, programmable decentralized computer.
If you're interested in the thesis driving Farcaster, this piece from @varunsrin lays it out.

items.rationale: Actively excited by fresh research-lab designs that make trustless markets and cheaper verification possible; favors elegant, obvious-in-retrospect mechanisms.

citations.quote: This is one of my favorite new protocol designs, fresh out of our research lab. With FDE, you could set up a trustless market to buy data.
Naysayer proofs can be extremely efficient... A really simple, obvious-in-retrospect design.

items.rationale: Pushes symbolic testing and new legal/technical safe-harbor tools to make crypto safer, treating safety as essential to the ecosystem's promise.

citations.quote: Crypto is unique, and fulfilling its promise requires developing new legal and technical tools for security researchers.
We hope Halmos will be the best symbolic testing tool in Ethereum.

items.rationale: Views smart accounts (preferred over 'smart wallet') as unlocking counter-intuitive new capabilities tied to identity and ownership.

citations.quote: Account abstraction enables smart accounts. What are some counter-intuitive possibilities enabled by smart accounts?
Smart account is much better than smart wallet... 'account' implies both identity and ownership — which is the truth.

items.rationale: Wants a composable rights layer so derivative works can be created and monetized with natural onchain attribution.

citations.quote: Recursive, networked creativity. Multiplayer for IP. Made possible with crypto.
Story Protocol fixes this with an onchain remix graph that makes composition and attribution natural.

items.rationale: Sees memecoin manias as zero-sum casino behavior that smart people rationalize via FOMO, distracting from crypto's real potential.

citations.quote: When memecoins have a moment, people who know better make tortured rationalizations... fomo can use the full intellect to justify itself with elaborate stories.
gm to people building crypto's real potential ☀️

items.rationale: Argues closed permissioned platforms cripple native crypto experiences and tax innovation; crypto payments can route around the ~30% cut.

citations.quote: Apple will cripple you, block you, or take your lunch if you try to offer a native experience.
If an app can collect payment from a mobile crypto wallet... merchants could save ~30%... The benefits of crypto start becoming very obvious.

items.rationale: Expects a 'mystery meat' internet where all content is assumed generated unless proven otherwise; motivates the provenance/crypto thesis.

citations.quote: we aren't far from the internet devolving into a place where *most* content has been generated (or can be generated on demand).
Eventually all content is assumed generated unless accompanied by the requisite provenance/signatures/attestations/proofs.

items.rationale: Criticizes restricting model weights and notes generative systems encode their makers' cultures, limiting openness and trust.

citations.quote: Democratizing knowledge has incredible benefits. If you believe this, why not share the LLaMA weights publicly?
Organizations which design generative AI systems are constrained produce designs that reflect the cultures of these organizations.

items.rationale: Warns simple p2p/federated designs without exit rights collapse back to functional centralization; architecture, not time, determines openness.

citations.quote: Because of its network architecture, Nostr likely degrades to centralization — like Fediverse. This happens often in simple p2p systems.
Federation doesn't necessarily include exit rights... Functionally centralized.

items.rationale: Believes LLMs already defeat CAPTCHA and can fabricate realistic histories, leaving networks exposed without proof-of-personhood.

citations.quote: CAPTCHA is done and LLMs today are already sophisticated enough to produce long and realistic looking 'histories' with minimal guidance from humans.

items.rationale: Sharply criticized Binance's proof-of-reserves as useless and exploitable; concern about exchange transparency.
items.age_note: Stated Nov 2022 around the FTX/Binance period; not restated since, so reflects that moment's stance.

citations.quote: Binance's system is useless and easily exploited by them. - Merkle tree wasn't shared... zero evidence they actually control the addresses included in the tree

items.rationale: Treats Ethereum as the core programmable decentralized computer; heavily invested in scaling it via L2s, light clients, and data-availability research.

citations.quote: I just contributed to the 🕯️ KZG Ceremony 🕯️ using lazzarin.eth to help scale Ethereum
danksharding and data availability sampling, which would radically decrease the cost of storing data on Ethereum

items.rationale: Sees Worldcoin's private sybil-resistance as a likely necessary component of any robust decentralized personhood system as AI breaks bot defenses.

citations.quote: Trust graph is much more robust coupled with a sybil resistance mechanism, which is what Worldcoin provides.
all networks need to integrate a private proof of personhood system like @worldnetwork?

items.rationale: Excited about the potential to make Bitcoin fully programmable and ZK-enabled (e.g., a VERIFY_SNARK opcode), reviving Satoshi's vision.
items.age_note: Excitement is conditional on protocol upgrades; paired with a security-model concern (see tokens_concerned).

citations.quote: adding a VERIFY_SNARK opcode to the bitcoin protocol — would make bitcoin totally programmable and free to move all over the internet.
We should bring zero knowledge back home to bitcoin.

items.rationale: Believes Bitcoin's long-term security model must change to survive coming decades, a structural risk to its durability absent upgrades.

citations.quote: Many have been doing this math — the security model of bitcoin needs to change if it's going to last the next few decades.

risk_regime.rationale: Strong builder optimism on crypto's technology and timing ('there has never been a better time to build'), driven by next-gen infra, improving regs, and evolving UX — but explicitly risk-off on speculation/memecoins, which he frames as casino distraction. Net posture is risk-on toward fundamental infrastructure and long-term value creation, not toward speculative trading.

notes: Eddy is an infrastructure/research-oriented a16z crypto engineer, not a markets/trading voice; his 'views' are about technology and protocol design more than token prices. Dominant and most-recent (Q1 2024) themes: ZK proving (Jolt/Lasso), content provenance vs deepfakes, proof of personhood, and a sharp distaste for memecoins. Recurring through-line across 18 months: centralization (Apple/closed platforms, degrading p2p networks) is the core enemy and 'crypto-the-computer' is the answer. Terminology note: he deliberately switched from 'Data Availability' to 'Data Publishing/Storage' in Sep 2023 — a vocabulary update, not a thesis reversal. No major stance reversals detected; older items (Binance PoR critique, LLaMA weights) are consistent with later views. Few specific tokens are discussed as investments; sectors/ecosystems dominate.
